data.title: Singapore's Growth Engines Remain Intact Despite Rising Energy Pressures
data.description:
By Deepali Bhargava, Regional Head of Research, Asia-Pacific

Singapore industrial output came in higher than expected

Industrial production recorded a strong upside surprise in March, expanding by 10.1% year-on-year, well above market expectations of around 6%. The surge was driven primarily by electronics output, which rose 30% YoY, almost entirely on the back of a sharp recovery in semiconductor production. Electronics accounted for the majority of the increase in industrial production during the month.

The strength in electronics production was underpinned by a pronounced acceleration in electronics exports, which jumped 74% YoY in March, lifting overall first quarter electronics export growth to 58% YoY. This performance is consistent with the strong momentum in AI-related exports observed in other Asian economies, including Taiwan and Korea.

Precision engineering output also remained robust, growing 14% YoY and ranking as the second-largest contributor to overall IP growth. The segment accounted for roughly 18% of total industrial production growth in March, underscoring the broadening momentum beyond semiconductors.

Impact of higher oil prices yet to be fully felt

High-frequency indicators suggest that activity in Singapore’s externally oriented sectors has so far remained broadly resilient following the onset of the Middle East conflict. In particular, port activity stayed above its historical average in March, pointing to limited near-term disruption to trade and logistics flows.

Both oil import and export growth turned positive in March, reversing the contraction seen in the months prior. This improvement likely reflects higher global oil prices. Notably, the oil trade balance slipped into deficit for the first time since November 2025. This suggests that while the drag from higher oil prices on economic activity has been relatively muted so far, pressures will start to surface and are likely to intensify over the coming quarters.

Operating costs have already risen across transportation, logistics, and manufacturing, leading to margin compression. According to the Monetary Authority of Singapore's (MAS) estimates, energy-related cost pressures are most pronounced in the wholesale trade sector, which accounts for around 19% of GDP. Roughly half of wholesale trade's input requirements are sourced from the energy-intensive transport and storage sector – particularly water transport services – making it especially vulnerable to sustained energy price shocks.

At the industry level, petroleum, gas and electricity, petrochemicals, basic chemicals, transportation, and water services are identified as the most energy-exposed sectors. In these industries, energy inputs account for more than 10% of total input requirements, implying limited capacity to absorb further increases in energy costs without a material impact on margins or downstream prices.

What this means for GDP growth

After a relatively strong first-quarter GDP growth of 4.6% YoY, we expect Singapore's GDP growth to moderate in the second half of the year as persistently higher energy prices weigh on costs and profitability.

That said, any slowdown is likely to be modest, as several offsetting factors remain in place. Policy measures aimed at cushioning household consumption should provide meaningful support, including utility rebates to offset higher electricity prices, cash handouts, and targeted sector-specific assistance. In addition, public capital expenditure continues to be a key pillar of growth. Large-scale projects – such as the development of Changi Airport Terminal 5, ongoing Housing & Development Board programmes, the construction of Cross Island Line Phase 2, and elevated defence spending – are helping to sustain overall economic momentum despite a more challenging external environment.

Within services, growth in the IT services segment (under the information and communications sector) has held up well, likely reflecting sustained demand for data hosting and digital infrastructure. Financial services have also remained resilient, as evidenced by the strength of equity markets and the Singapore dollar. The SGD continues to stand out as one of the few Asian currencies to post positive year-to-date returns, underscoring its perception as a relative safe haven amid heightened global uncertainty.

Overall, we maintain our relatively strong GDP growth target of 3.3% for 2026.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more.

data.title: Gold As A Fiscal Barometer: Why Deficits Matter More Than Rates
data.description:
Thesis

Gold is not driven by the variables most commonly cited in market narratives. Factors such as real interest rates, inflation, and central bank policy fail to provide a consistent or reliable framework for explaining long-term gold price movements across different macro regimes. Their influence is better understood as conditional and transmission-based rather than structural.

We argue that the more stable and explanatory variable is fiscal sustainability, specifically the trajectory of the US budget deficit. Expanding deficits increase refinancing pressure, raise questions around the long-term credibility of sovereign balance sheets, and indirectly shape the environment in which monetary policy operates. In such a framework, gold functions as a market-based assessment of fiscal credibility rather than a direct reaction to individual monetary variables.

As the US fiscal position continues to deteriorate, with a rising net interest burden and a large share of debt requiring near-term refinancing, the structural backdrop becomes increasingly supportive of higher gold prices. For this reason, we maintain a long-term constructive view and rate gold as a STRONG BUY.

Testing the Monetary Narrative

We have been bullish on Gold Spot Price (XAUUSD:CUR) and SPDR Gold Shares ETF (GLD) for some time (here), but we felt we needed to dive much deeper into the factors affecting the price of gold since gold does not produce any cash flows. A casual search on the internet or a prompt to your favorite AI model will flag a weaker dollar, central bank reserves, gold mining, safe haven demand, inflation, real interest rates, and consumer demand for jewelry, or, more in modern times, things like trade concerns (here) (here).

Although jewelry accounts for the largest share of physical gold demand, it does not set the price because it reacts to price changes rather than driving them. Gold prices are determined by fast, capital-intensive flows such as ETFs, central banks, and futures markets, which act as the marginal buyers.

However, we argue that dollar movements, inflation, and real interest rates are not direct factors that direct the trend of the price of gold over the long term. We believe that these variables fail to act as consistent and reliable predictors of long-term gold price movements and are better understood as transmission channels rather than primary drivers. On a short-term, daily to monthly basis, we acknowledge that there can be multiple factors acting in opposite directions that have absolutely no effect over the long term or that exhibit limited predictive power when evaluated across full market cycles.

We find it interesting that some of the largest banks still (here) still see real yield as a predictor of gold price trend. While real yields may explain certain shorter-term dynamics, our analysis suggests that their relationship with gold is unstable and highly regime-dependent over longer horizons. We present data on a large enough scale that show that real interest rates are not a predictor of gold price movements.

As can be seen from the chart above, there are times when gold prices rise along with real yield, like in the 1970s or over the last couple of quarters. However, over the majority of the period, like 1982-2000 or 2010-2016, the price of gold fell along with the real yield. Then there are periods like 2000-2010, where gold rises sharply with real yield going down sharply.

How about real interest rate expectations?

We find that the connection here is even weaker, with real interest rate expectations going up and down in the 2000-2010 period, while the gold price was on the rise.

Also, intuitively, data show that gold prices are not driven by monetary policy.

As can be seen from the chart above, there is no correlation between the movement of the Federal Funds Target rate and the gold price. Also, there is no correlation between gold price movements during the US recession (highlighted on the chart).

Then there is the size of the FED balance sheet.

Intuitively speaking, investors would expect that with a growing balance sheet, the price of gold would rise. That did happen throughout most of the 2000-2014 period. However, the largest growth of the gold price happened during the FED balance sheet contraction over the last three to four years. In our view, the presented data suggest that monetary policy variables do not provide a consistent framework for explaining long-term gold price trends, particularly when analyzed across different macro regimes.

However, there is one factor that seems to determine the movement of the gold price over the long term quite consistently: the US budget deficit as a percentage of GDP.

As can be seen by the chart above, a long period of relatively stable gold prices, 1983-2006, corresponds with a period of budget deficit that seemed to be under control. In short periods where the deficit grew more sharply (the line is going down on the chart), we can observe gold prices rising, but as soon as the deficit reverses, so do gold prices. However, with a sharp budget deficit, the price of gold tends to rise sharply. But, please note the COVID area where, during the sharpest budget deficit, the price of gold fell.

This may reflect market expectations that the fiscal expansion would prove temporary, rather than a structural deterioration in fiscal sustainability. However, as the deficit began to rise and as expectations of the deficit began to rise, i.e., expecting a larger budget deficit, the price of gold surged. This pattern appears broadly consistent across multiple historical periods, although not without exceptions.

So, in our view, gold is a pricing of fiscal sustainability, while monetary variables are transmission channels rather than primary drivers. In that sense, gold can be interpreted as a market-based assessment of sovereign fiscal credibility rather than a direct reaction to individual monetary variables.

Our View

As we concluded, the view on the gold price should be derived from the view on the movement of the US budget deficit. As presented in the following chart, the net interest burden, i.e., interest expense as part of the budget in 2025, was 14%, topping the defense.

Here, it is important to keep in mind that almost 1/3 of the total debt of the United States is maturing in a year, as can be seen by the following chart.

The current average fixed coupon of 3.02% is considerably under the current curve, which means that only refinancing of the current debt would add to the total net interest burden. If we add a top to that expected deficit of 5.8% of GDP in 2026, it looks like net interest will grow as a part of GDP, leaving even less room for cutting the deficit without causing a severe recession. Under such a framework, fiscal dynamics increasingly point toward structural pressure on sovereign balance sheets, which historically has coincided with stronger gold price environments. This is why we are bullish on gold and would rate it as a STRONG BUY. In that view, we rate as a STRONG BUY: Gold Spot Price (XAUUSD:CUR), SPDR Gold Shares ETF (GLD), iShares Gold Trust ETF (IAU), GraniteShares Gold Trust ETF (BAR), VanEck Merk Gold ETF (OUNZ), and Sprott Physical Gold Trust USD (PHYS:CA).

Risk to Our Bullish Thesis

Since the rising budget deficit as a % of GDP is our main impetus for a long-term bullish view, we think that any demonstration of a smaller budget deficit or a sharp move to a more neutral budget would be a risk for the gold price. We note that the deficit as a percent of GDP can be cut in both ways, as follows. First, cutting the deficit itself in absolute terms by spending less or by growing the economy much faster than the deficit, which can be accomplished by severe technological breakthroughs in the field of AI, energy production, or other parts of the economy.

Also, we note the possibility of a sharp short-term correction, like we saw this year, since there is a lot of noise on the market regarding the factors driving the long-term price of gold.

To Sum It Up

Gold is often framed through the lens of monetary variables, from real yields to central bank policy. However, the evidence presented suggests that these factors do not offer a stable or consistent explanation for long-term price movements. Instead, they appear as transmission mechanisms rather than primary drivers.

What emerges more consistently is a relationship between gold and the sustainability of fiscal policy, particularly the US budget deficit. Gold, in this sense, is not merely an inflation hedge or a reaction to interest rates but a market-based assessment of long-term fiscal credibility.

As deficits expand and refinancing pressures build, the system increasingly relies on continued demand for sovereign debt at acceptable yields. When that confidence is questioned, gold becomes a natural alternative.

This is why our long-term view on gold remains constructive. Not because of any single monetary variable, but because the structural trajectory of fiscal policy continues to deteriorate.

We rate gold as a STRONG BUY.

data.title: Bank of America Preferreds Pair Trade Idea
data.description:
Co-authored by Relative Value.

Overview

The idea here is pretty similar to the JPMorgan preferreds pair trade we recently posted. However, this time it will be about Bank of America's (BAC) listed preferred stocks - the 4.125% Depositary Shares Non-Cumulative Preferred Stock Series PP (BAC.PR.P) as the long leg in the pair trade, and the 6.00% Depositary Shares Non-Cumulative Preferred Stock Series GG (BAC.PR.B) as the short one. With proper sizing, this pair trade should carry no credit risk, so investors seeking safer exposure can find it, or the idea behind it, interesting.

Bank of America Overview

Founded in 1904 and headquartered in Charlotte, North Carolina, Bank of America Corporation is a bank and financial holding company. It operates on a global scale, and by asset size, it is the second-largest bank in the US. Through its segments, the corporation provides a wide variety of financial products and services to its clients both in the US and around the globe. The scale of the bank is so large that its healthy operation is considered of systemic importance for the US financial system, so it is a part of the "Too Big to Fail" list.

The chart below depicts how the investors have evaluated the common stock of the bank BAC for the past ten years:

To get a better idea of the bank's performance over this period, we'll compare its total return to that of the general market, represented by the S&P 500, and to the average for the US banking sector, represented by State Street SPDR S&P Bank ETF (KBE):

Over the observed period, BAC's total return has followed the general trend of the S&P 500 and the banking sector, but has managed to outperform both by a considerable amount.

Looking at Bank of America's direct peers - the largest US diversified banks—we see that BAC lags only JPMorgan, and this is only since 2023. Before that, BAC's and JPM's total returns were rather correlated:

As for the expected future performance of BAC, it looks bright, according to most of the analysts who cover the bank. The majority of the SA authors are bullish in their expectations, giving BAC a "Buy" rating, while most of the Wall Street analysts and the Quant Rating score it even higher with a "Strong Buy":

P/E Ratio and EPS Growth Rate

Besides looking at historical performance compared to peers, to assess BAC's investment qualities, we can have a look at the company's current valuation, and what the forward expectations about it are telling us.

Bank of America's TTM P/E ratio is 12.88, and the forward value is expected to drop to around 11.66. A lower forward P/E ratio indicates increased expected earnings. However, the expected forward value for the sector median is even lower, indicating that despite Bank of America's earnings being expected to grow, it will be to a lower degree than the average peer.

The earnings growth rate after the expected peak in 2026 is also showing signs of weakness, as the consensus EPS estimate above shows. It is still a positive expectation for growth in earnings, so I cannot give the common stock a negative score. However, the expected earnings growth at a lower rate after 2026 and the sector median offering the investors a lower P/E mean that I cannot give a "Buy" score either. As of the current moment, I remain neutral on the investment qualities of the common stock BAC, scoring it a "Hold" rating.

Capital Structure

Following is the capital structure of Bank of America as of the First Quarter of 2026. For comparison reasons, the capital structure for the previous four quarters is provided too:

As of the end of March 2026, Bank of America had approximately $781 billion in debt, senior to the $25 billion in preferred equity. At the lowest position in the capital structure is the common equity of close to $276 billion.

As of the end of Q1'26, Bank of America has a Common Equity Tier 1 under the Basel 3 Capital requirements of 11.2%. This is a 20 basis point drop compared to the ratio at the end of 2025 and 60 basis points lower than a year ago. There is still a cushion above the mandatory CET 1 capital requirement of 10% for Bank of America, but any long-term investor in the bank's products should keep an eye on this metric.

Dividends

Bank of America has a rather impressive history of 31 years of uninterrupted dividends for common stockholders, the last 12 of which have been consistently increasing:

The TTM dividend distributed to BAC's shareholders amounts to $1.10 in quarterly payments. With the current price of the stock at $52.43, that corresponds to a dividend yield of 2.10%. This is how the dividend yield has looked for the past 10 years:

And the forward estimations for the dividend and dividend yield:

Bank of America has approximately 7.4 billion common shares outstanding, and with a $1.12 forward estimated annual payment per share, we can calculate around $8.3 billion forward annualized distribution for the common stockholders. The total of $1.5 billion annual dividend for the preferred shares of the bank represents approximately 18% of that sum. The cushion for the preferreds' distributions is large enough. I do not see any particular danger for them in the foreseeable future.

Company Credibility

Fitch Ratings - New York - 03 Jun 2025: Fitch Ratings has affirmed Bank of America Corporation's (BAC) Long- and Short-Term Issuer Default Rating (IDRs) at 'AA-' and 'F1+', respectively. In addition, Fitch has affirmed Bank of America, N.A.'s (BANA) Long- and Short-Term IDRs at 'AA' and 'F1+', respectively. The Rating Outlook on the Long-Term IDRs is Stable. A full list of rating actions appears below.

Key Rating Drivers

Strong Intrinsic Profile: BAC's intrinsic creditworthiness, as expressed by its Viability Rating (VR) of 'aa-', drives its IDRs. The VR reflects BAC's leading franchise in many of its core businesses, its diversified business mix and consistent strategy, which Fitch believes will continue to underpin a more stable through-the-cycle financial performance relative to peers. BAC's VR is one notch above the implied VR of 'a+' due to the company's business profile, which Fitch assesses at 'aa-' and considers a significant factor in its ratings.

This is the beginning of the recent rating action report with which Fitch affirms the 'AA-' long-term issuer default rating with a stable outlook for Bank of America. The rating history of the bank for the past couple of years is shown below. The last credit rating change was in June 2021, when the score was upgraded from 'A+' to the current 'AA-'.

The pair trade idea

Bank of America has a number of listed preferred stocks. In this case, we seek a pair trade opportunity among the fixed-rate ones, so we've listed them below, along with their current metrics:

All of these preferreds, except for BAC-L, have an embedded call option. Due to the difference in the nominal yields of each issue, the call options have different values. The call option is exercisable by the company, so it has a negative value for the holders of the particular stock. Under normal circumstances, the investors will want a higher compensation for holding a security with a higher redemption probability than with a lower one. To put it in simple words, the high coupon preferreds like BAC-B and BAC-K will normally be priced with higher CY than the low nominals of the family—BAC-P, BAC-Q, and BAC-O. Anytime we see a deviation from this rule of thumb, we know a mispricing is happening, and we start watching for a potential pair trade to try to profit from the mean reversion.

The chart shows the CY spread between the largest and the smallest nominal yield listed for preferreds of Bank of America with $25 liquidation preference—BAC-B and BAC-P. The chart shows that BAC-B was priced at an average of 52 basis points higher current yield than BAC-P's current yield for the time both of them were listed on the exchange. Periodically, a mispricing due to selling pressure in the fixed income universe happens, and it is up to the pair traders to try to profit from it on the mean reversion.

We can show the mispricing in another way, too. By stripping the call option and its effect on each security's current yield, we can get the option-adjusted yield of each preferred. As discussed above, the small nominal yielders BAC-P, BAC-O, and BAC-Q are currently undervalued compared to BAC-B and BAC-K. The calculation shows that we have a mispricing of 45-50 basis points of CY between each of the three low-coupon preferreds and the two high-coupon ones. In the particular case of BAC-P and BAC-B, 47 BP of CY corresponds to close to $1.50 potential per paired preferreds until a mean reversion.

How Can One Play This Out

For this article's consideration purpose, I've picked the lowest nominal yielder of the family, BAC-P, as the long leg in the pair trade and the highest nominal yielder, BAC-B, as the short leg. The metrics above show that there are other possible ways to play this opportunity out, so it will all depend on what your broker is offering you as borrowing fees, borrowing availability, etc.

These are important things to factor in and keep an eye on as the pair trade develops, as in an unfavorable scenario, they can erode the pair trade potential.

As an example, BAC-B's borrowing fee was around 3% for a long time, but currently it has increased to 5%. At 5%, it will cost me approximately $0.10 per share per month to hold the short position open, and I have to keep this in mind, as this erodes the trade potential with time. If the borrowing fee changes, I'll have to reevaluate how it affects the pair trade and whether it is still viable.

Conclusion

This is a straightforward pair trade idea between the same type of products of the same issuer, which presents itself periodically, when selling pressure hits the fixed-income vehicles. If taken with equal dollar value in both legs, the superposition should carry no credit risk. Pair trading, however, brings another type of risk, as described above. "Take the trade and forget about it" is not a viable concept here, as it requires a little bit of babysitting. This makes it not a suitable pick for everyone. Investors' discretion is required.

data.title: Qualcomm Soars On OpenAI Deal, But It's A Profit-Taking Opportunity (Downgrade)
data.description:
QUALCOMM Incorporated (QCOM) soared 12% on Monday, April 27. It’s the latest semiconductor industry stock to jump during what has been a historic chip rally. The iShares Semiconductor ETF (SOXX) is up 18 days running coming into April’s final sessions. A perfect 22-for-22 trading-day April is quite possible, with the VanEck Semiconductor ETF (SMH) rising another 2% to kick off this week.

But why is QCOM on the rise today? It was major news reported by TF International Securities analyst Ming-Chi Kuo, who wrote that OpenAI (OPENAI) is exploring a significant move into the hardware space. According to the analyst, OpenAI is collaborating with both Qualcomm and MediaTek to develop custom smartphone processors. QCOM traded up 12% on Monday morning.

I had a Buy rating on QCOM in early March, following a solid Q1 report. Today, I’m downgrading the stock to a hold due to valuation. The tie-up with the OpenAI report has fuzzy details, and any earnings accretion would be in the out years. Today, I’ll offer color on QCOM heading into its Q2 report on April 29th, share more on the latest OpenAI news, and provide a technical update.

QCOM +3% YoY Coming Into Its Earnings Week

Semiconductors Cheaper vs. Last October

OpenAI may look to team up with Qualcomm to develop a smartphone, a move that had been anticipated (particularly after Jony Ive departed Apple Inc. (AAPL)), but today’s new details that Qualcomm is a potential partner fueled the semiconductor rally. It's important to underscore that, according to the report, mass production for OpenAI devices is not expected until 2028.

Zooming out, I see QCOM’s risk/reward as simply more balanced today. Shares have soared from $122 to near $170. It's now at the intrinsic value target I laid out back in the first quarter. I don’t see much reason to ratchet up my profit forecast for the now-$175 billion market-cap chip company. Yes, there were very bullish reports from the likes of Texas Instruments Incorporated (TXN) and Intel Corporation (INTC) last week, along with a solid up-move in fellow automotive semiconductor player ON Semiconductor Corporation (ON) (see my recent analysis here).

Still, QCOM’s core smartphone business could benefit from an OpenAI partnership, but we need more details and confirmation that sales and earnings will be boosted by the ChatGPT developer. All the while, QCOM faces rising memory costs that could actually cool global smartphone demand in the quarters ahead (something the market seems to ignore for now). For the firm itself, Qualcomm could lose market share to MediaTek among Chinese Android manufacturers and the ongoing loss of Apple as it transitions to its internal modem solutions.

Looking ahead to the Q2 report, the market prices in a high 7.8% earnings-related stock price swing based on the at-the-money straddle expiring soonest after Wednesday night’s report. The average actual swing is 6.3%, and implied volatility on QCOM is high at 55%. Keep in mind that the stock has traded lower post-earnings in the past seven instances, according to data from Option Research & Technology Services.

QCOM: Weak Earnings Reactions Lately

The Q2 report will be a critical test of the smartphone market’s health. We could get details on a decline in 2026 smartphone sales, which is a negative headline risk. Such a story could bring investors down to reality following Monday’s euphoric reaction to the OpenAI report.

As it stands, EPS estimates for the current year have actually fallen since my previous analysis. FY 2026 operating per-share earnings are now seen at $11.08, down from $11.19 in early March. Out-year EPS is also lower… $11.02 from $11.25. Indeed, there have been a high of 26 sell-side downgrades in the past 90 days, compared to a lone upgrade.

Qualcomm: Revenue & Earnings Forecasts, EPS Revision Trends

Goldman Sachs notes that QCOM’s share price tends to follow EPS estimates. Hence, the current rally to near $170 counters the profit outlook correlation. I see that as a trend that simply cannot hold.

QCOM: Generally Tracks EPS Forecasts

On valuation, I stick with my $169 number from March. Assuming $11.25 of normalized EPS over the next 12 months and applying a 15x P/E, shares should be just shy of $170.

QCOM: Closer to Fair Value

Competitor Analysis

Looking ahead, corporate event data provided by Wall Street Horizon shows a confirmed Q2 2026 earnings date of Wednesday, April 29th, AMC.

It’s a busy calendar in the back half of the quarter, with the company presenting at the Bernstein 42nd Annual Strategic Decisions Conference (SDC) 2026 on May 27 and Qualcomm hosting its Annual General Meeting on Wednesday, June 24.

Corporate Event Risk Calendar

The Technical Take

With shares jumping big ahead of the Q2 report, QCOM’s technical situation is mixed. Notice in the chart below that the stock has rallied through the long-term 200-day moving average. But the climb only brings QCOM to the apex of a consolidation pattern that unfolded from 2024 to this past January. Indeed, there’s a high amount of volume by price up to $170. So, a pause here (and possibly a pullback after earnings) would certainly make sense.

Moreover, take a look at the RSI momentum oscillator at the top of the graph. On the 2/bar-per-day chart, the 14-period RSI is now at an extreme 86. That also augurs for either a price dip or a correction through time, underscoring my downgrade to a hold.

QCOM: Shares Approach Resistance, Extreme RSI

The Bottom Line

I have a Hold rating on QCOM. A report indicating an OpenAI-Qualcomm partnership is the latest semiconductor industry bullish news. I’m taking a more measured stance, given the intense April chip rally and ahead of Qualcomm’s Q2 report due out Wednesday night.

data.title: Regeneron: Catching Another Wind
data.description:
Our last article about Regeneron (REGN) seems awkwardly timed in hindsight, given we called for even more upside from their all-time high when not a month later they entered a slow-but-steep selloff, no doubt exacerbated by the onslaught of issues around approval delays, political pressure around drug pricing, and the exogenous market shock of the US tariff announcements:

Having reviewed all of the recent data, though, nothing much has really changed from where we’re sitting. If anything, the business looks as well-positioned as ever in terms of their product portfolio. They have a clearly articulated roadmap, new drugs entering the market with high prospects, and the indomitable force that is George Yancopoulos still running the R&D. While we can’t say that everything looks like smooth sailing right now, there’s a lot more positive momentum from this position than we’ve seen in a while, so it’s time once again for us to review the puts and takes and explain why we believe the company has managed to stoke the fire over the last six months.

Recent Product Developments

Commercial

The roughest part of late 2024 into 2025 for us was seeing how often Regeneron struggled to get their NDAs approved because of issues with the fill/finish facilities for multiple unrelated drugs. Eylea, linvoseltamab, and odronextamab all had regulatory delays throughout 2025 caused by these third-party facilities. Most of the issues seem to be clearing up, though: linvoseltamab has been approved as Lynozyfic in the US and Europe, and Eylea HD’s prefilled syringe formulation has an approved alternate filler with an expected decision date from the FDA any day now. Odronextamab hasn’t been discussed in the last six months or so and isn’t listed on the 2026 “select milestones” in the Q4 press release, most likely because Lynozyfic is getting all of the attention as the breakthrough bispecific antibody in heme-onc (and beyond). There’s no reason to suspect it won’t make it to market, but the timeline is uncertain, especially if addressing the fill/finish issues isn’t as straightforward as it was for Eylea.

As for the on-market efforts, things seem to be moving at a good pace, although whether the portfolio is accelerating is certainly up for debate. Eylea HD was approved for a new indication, retinal vein occlusion (RVO), and for monthly dosing, one of the “killer features” of this high-dose formulation that the company was touting. With the last “quality of life” improvement coming in the form of the prefilled syringe, Eylea HD can hopefully start to pull away from the rest of the pack in ophthalmology, but from where we’re sitting, this is solidly “legacy asset management.” Calling VEGF-mediated eye diseases a “solved problem” is perhaps a touch dramatic, but with the success that low-dose Eylea, Avastin, and other biosimilars have had, this field is mostly picked over unless, as we’ve said in the past, someone comes out with something “way better” (longer-acting, reduced risk of complications) or “way cheaper” (cost of production is pretty much the only lever here).

Dupixent, meanwhile, continues to steamroll everything they throw at it. The newest wins include label expansions for chronic spontaneous urticaria (CSU) in Europe (including for pediatric patients) and allergic fungal rhinosinusitis in the US, bringing its total number of approved indications to nine, only two behind adalimumab, another blockbuster immune-modifying therapy with zero overlap in conditions covered. With the Sanofi payback deal set to run off soon, a $1.2 billion run rate coming into 2026, and at least five years of patent protection still ahead, according to leadership based on their remarks from the TD Cowen Health Care Conference at the beginning of March, it’s easy to see why everyone is banking on it as a major driver for the foreseeable future. On our charts, it’s squarely in the “money now” category, occupying the spot Eylea once held, providing the revenue they need to keep turning the R&D engine, now at a higher gear compared to Eylea’s run.

While we were a bit unkind towards Libtayo early on in our coverage of Regeneron, it has quietly proven itself to be a true contender in its domains. Although it’s still primarily focused on indications in skin cancer and lung cancer, management commentary suggests it’s quickly becoming a “preferred immunotherapy,” especially for patients naive to other immunotherapy treatments. The differentiator for Libtayo versus the other PD-1-based therapies is Regeneron’s platform VelocImmune, their vaunted system for screening and producing fully humanized antibodies in a fraction of the time compared to previous biological therapeutic technologies. Chief Science Officer George Yancopoulos discussed this “X-factor” during his appearance at the J.P. Morgan Healthcare Conference, albeit in reference to Dupixent, but the argument extends to their entire antibody portfolio (emphasis ours):

A lot of people have a mistaken belief that it's easy to make antibodies and all antibodies are created the same … more fully human antibodies have been approved from our platform than any other platform, 10% to 20% of all approved antibodies come from our platform.

And we repeatedly are producing the best agents with the least number of problems such as ADA, antidrug antibodies and so forth. And so when you have 2 antibodies against the same target, they're not necessarily the same. And across many programs. Remember, other people have tried to make DUPIXENT like antibodies that all failed, including Amgen. So it's not that easy to always make the best antibodies. Our platform and our people have perfected that ability. And that's why our antibodies seem to work in places where other antibodies against the same targets don't work.

Derek can attest that the process of producing a biological therapeutic that faithfully “mimics” a natural human antibody is exceptionally difficult, as it involves more than just the protein sequence. Libtayo’s growing presence and its go-to status in early lines of treatment underscore this edge that Regeneron brings to their portfolio.

And speaking of edges based on this platform, Lynozyfic, the latest entry to the stable, is already showing signs of being a next-generation “pipeline in a product” for the company. Management has said that Lynozyfic is showing almost double the complete response rate as other therapeutics in multiple myeloma (to wit, these would be Elrexfio (PFE) and Tecvayli/Talvey (JNJ)) with fewer complications. We can't think of a more textbook example of “way better” when it comes to disrupting the established players in the space. This drug is an absolute game changer in the heme-onc space, showcasing the true power of bispecifics, and this may only be the beginning for Lynozyfic in particular.

Clinical Trials

On the late-stage clinical trial side, there’s a decent amount to unpack for three major branches of research: oncology, immunology, and gene therapies. Most of the recent attention has been on fianlimab, the LAG-3 inhibitor the company is pairing with Libtayo as a combination therapy in the same vein as Bristol-Myers’ (BMY) Opdualag. The company has stated they are fully blinded to the results of the ongoing Phase 3 clinical trial in advanced metastatic melanoma, using Keytruda (MRK) as the comparison, and although a readout is anticipated sometime in the first half of this year, delays have made analysts wonder if there is cause for concern. At the Leerink Global Healthcare Conference, head of IR Ryan Crowe made the following statement:

We are totally blinded to the events and where they're occurring. We only know the total number and how they are accruing at the rate in which they're accruing. So we will see. I think you can look at it from 2 different ways. On the negative side, pembrolizumab is doing something miraculous and amazing and it's going to have a much higher-than-anticipated median PFS or you could look at it and say, well, the active arms are actually doing quite well and are approaching the activity that we saw in the early human studies where pooled median PFS across 3 independent cohorts was 24 months.

Crowe went on to comment that the trial was designed with power in such a way that even if Keytruda outperformed its historical median for progression-free survival in first-line metastatic melanoma, they would be okay. Our read on this continued scrutiny of the trial and the deflections based on “we are blinded to the detailed results” doesn't inspire much confidence. Hopefully this isn’t “herd mentality” thinking, but from a business perspective, trials dragging on and lacking that “show-stopper” power eventually make you lose interest and wander on to the next thing.

And speaking of “the next thing,” this seems like a good place to talk about the current state of the gene therapy landscape that Regeneron is supporting. There are three major treatments that jumped out at us in reviewing the literature from the last year or so.

First, the big one: the adverse events around nex-z, the treatment for a form of amyloidosis in partnership with Intellia (NTLA). Even as the clinical holds begin to lift, this incident underscores the reality of gene-editing treatments that the research community has to have in the backs of their heads, given that folks remember the tragic fatalities all too readily versus the more numerous but less “shocking” benign-to-positive cases. From our perspective, this project is done and dusted; nothing more to see here. We treat this as a zero for all intents and purposes.

Next, the deep collaboration with Alnylam (ALNY) on siRNA-based gene silencing treatments. While the science is sound and the first treatments are looking promising (more on the tentpole asset cemdisiran later), news on their other treatments hasn’t been flowing as steadily, which makes it hard to assess how much of a tail there is to the returns of this partnership from Regeneron’s end.

Last, the deal in December with Tessera Therapeutics to develop their gene editing therapy for alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency. While it’s still early days (and Tessera is running the initial portion of the trials), our reads are that a) Regeneron believes in gene editing therapies as an eventually-legitimate medicinal modality, and b) they want more partners in this space, either because they see that much opportunity or because their current partnerships aren’t adequate. Given the situation with Intellia, we’re leaning towards the latter.

One last area that seems worth calling out, especially as we transition to the next section, is what’s currently going on in the immunology space. Lynozyfic and Dupixent are putting in work here, both showing promise as part of therapies for food allergies, alongside new molecules in Phase 3 trials for birch and cat allergies. As severe allergy sufferers ourselves, anything that helps us to better understand the immune system and alleviate the dysfunctions that come with its incredible power is a welcome development. However, the itepekimab trials seem to have come back mixed, leaving Regeneron and Sanofi (SNY), their partner on the asset, in the lurch about how to proceed. Ryan Crowe said at the TD Cowen Annual Healthcare Conference that they were weighing their options, but we’ll probably have to wait until the next earnings call to hear what, if anything, they decide to do. Our take: it’s a long shot; we're not going to bank on anything for the time being.

The Road Ahead

As strong as the current portfolio is, our angle for holding Regeneron is and always has been about their innovative capabilities, and at this point in time, the innovation is looking as healthy as we’ve ever seen it.

During the co-founders’ appearance at the JP Morgan Healthcare Conference and subsequently on the Q4 earnings call, they explicitly named five “key late-stage programs” the company is focused on for the near-to-medium term: solid tumor oncology, myeloma, complement-mediated diseases, anticoagulation, and obesity. These come as no surprise to us, having heard all of these therapies mentioned in earnings calls and conference appearances over the last few years, but this is the first time they’ve named these “pillars” as a strategic focus alongside the obvious tentpoles of ophthalmology and I&I.

Going down the list in order of how big a splash we see each making:

Obesity

Novo’s (NVO) CEO said it not too long ago, the weight-loss drug “boom” is only getting started. While the hotness du jour is oral GLP-1 agonists, both the Wegovy formulation and Lilly’s (LLY) newly approved oral GLP-1 agonist orforglipron (mercifully now given a more easily pronounced brand name of Foundayo), Regeneron has long been taking the more nuanced position of focusing on more than just “raw” weight loss as the answer to obesity.

Now, they are looking to pair their GLP-1 agonist olatorepatide in conjunction with their heavy-duty cholesterol medication Praluent, targeting the comorbidity of cardiovascular disease and high cholesterol with a single dose analogous to the existing subcutaneous GLP-1 agonist treatments. This tactic is a brilliant way to protect and expand Praluent’s share of a market projected to grow as much as 19% annually over the next decade, but it is also a bit of a gamble that physicians would be willing to prescribe PCSK9 inhibitors over other types of cholesterol management therapies (of which there are many), making us wonder what share of the market this combination therapy would actually reach.

Complement-mediated disease

This pillar surprised us, but with projections to grow at nearly 16% annually to a market size of over $270 billion, it’s no surprise everyone is scrambling to cash in on it. We like how Regeneron is positioned in this market, as they are on track for their antibody pozelimab (already approved as Veopoz for a rare genetic condition, CHAPLE syndrome, related to an immune system dysfunction), in combination with Alnylam’s siRNA therapeutic cemdisiran, to be submitted and approved by the end of the year for generalized myasthenia gravis (gMG), with the other major conditions—geographic atrophy (GA) and paroxysmal nocturnal hemoglobinuria (PNH)—not far behind. With their established presence in ophthalmology, pushing the treatment alongside Eylea shouldn’t be hard to do, and their track record in other immunological conditions (plus the existing approval of pozelimab) should give them a strong head start on the other conditions. Reading through the conference call they hosted recently, on the back of the announcement that their Phase III gMG trial met primary and secondary endpoints, there is a lot of “pipeline in a product” potential here, and we’re excited to see what it can bring.

Myeloma/Solid Tumor

Given these are the most mature of the new pillars, it’s weird to be ranking them so low (and basically equal), but again, we’re more in it for the innovation. Our thinking involves both the heavy investment in the space and the lack of maturity in the pipeline relative to the other pillars. It’s something of a dead heat between the work on fianlimab, odronextamab, and Lynozyfic in terms of how this space will be defined in the near term for Regeneron. The medium term may well be more defined by the solid tumor work, with their Phase 2 trials ongoing for various conditions such as ovarian and prostate cancer.

Anticoagulation

As they continue to push the standard of care for bleeding disorders, going deeper into the mechanisms of the pathway and “shifting left” the treatment cycle from recovery to prevention, the fact remains that the market is a bit smaller here than for the other new pillars, with predicted growth of about 12% annually and a pretty packed field of participants, even with the idea that prevention will require constant dosing.

If you put the whole portfolio together, you can bucket each asset/franchise along the lines of “transformative versus incremental” and “established versus emerging” in terms of the tech. Here’s our breakdown of where Regeneron is currently situated:

The consensus seems to be focused on the top-left quadrant, which is where Eylea used to be until Dupixent came along; now they still only see one tentpole asset while downplaying the vast amount of work bubbling under the surface. Libtayo has the potential to become a tentpole, but fianlimab will have a huge say in its fate. The obesity franchise is stuck in the middle as well, between the “lifecycle management” move with Praluent, coming in late to the party with their own GLP-1 agonist while also trying to position trevogrumab as the kicker that will give the business legitimate tentpole status. Lynozyfic is now slowly transitioning from “future blockbuster” to “current tentpole,” as cemdisiran gears up to make the same jump as early as the end of this year.

Lastly, a word about the “Long Bets” quadrant. Here, the play is less about the “10x grade” revenue—consider that the diseases being targeted currently are all rare and/or neglected—but more about having a ground truth against which they can test out these highly experimental therapeutic modalities (and work out the major kinks) before rolling them out into the therapeutic areas with larger markets, thus moving them into “Future Blockbusters” territory.

Lingering Concerns

Confession time: we’re mortal, and watching Regeneron drop 50% in the span of nine months was nauseating, regardless of cost basis. It’s definitely worth taking a moment to rewind the tape to see how much of that drop was macro-driven versus the company’s own doing.

Regulation

While the political discourse has shifted considerably in recent months, make no mistake, drug pricing has still been fingered as one of the root causes of increasing cost-of-living in the US, which is bringing the big pharma companies under pressure once again. The policy solution du jour has come in the form of “most favored nation” pricing agreements with the US government, and Regeneron just entered into such a deal, one of the last major pharma companies to do so. The fact that it coincides with FDA approval for their genetic-based hearing loss therapy Otarmeni is impossible to ignore, as is the announcement that they will provide the therapy for free to “clinically eligible individuals in the U.S.”

The most material impact of the agreement is the repricing of Regeneron’s portfolio for Medicaid patients; how this will actually affect the bottom line is uncertain, given the reference price is not known, but it’s safe to assume it will take a bite out of the profits of the more broadly prescribed therapies, namely Dupixent. The proverbial carrot of relief from “future pricing mandates” and tariff relief may ease the blow, especially with several new products starting to build steam, but there’s no guarantee this “handshake agreement” doesn’t eventually become binding legislation, which is desired by the executive branch and would present a sizable headwind to the medium-to-long-term prospects.

Industry Disruption

Regeneron is now in the unfortunate position of going from being a “disruptor” in the market to a mainstay at risk of being disrupted itself. Their proprietary antibody platform and the trove of genetic data do still give them an edge, but the pace of development in biotech is changing drastically. Between “AI-native” biotech companies (e.g., Isomorphic Labs (GOOG), Absci (ABSI)) and frontier AI research labs pushing into biological research (Anthropic’s acquisition of Coefficient Bio, Nvidia’s (NVDA) Proteina-Complexa for protein binder design), the pressure is on. These upstarts aren’t just playing penny ante with the drugs, either; Coefficient was founded by former members of Genentech’s (RHHBY) in-house computational biology wing, and they have the pedigree needed to compete and disrupt, even in Regeneron’s own backyard of antibody-based therapeutics. We’ve said before that the road from lead compound to approved product is long and arduous, and to date we still have not heard of a fully AI-integrated drug making it through to market any faster or cheaper than non-AI-based drugs, so for now the threat has yet to materialize, but its advent draws ever closer.

Succession

Way back in our first article about Regeneron, we commented on how there wasn’t much in the way of a stated plan for the company after the co-founders had left. Three years later, there has been no additional clarity on this point, while they still tout George Yancopoulos, age 66, as their Chief Science Officer and the reason their capital allocation strategy continues to be to invest in themselves. Granted, more of the company’s VPs and SVPs are getting spotlights through conference appearances and the company’s own roundtable series, and it would be outlandish to suggest a biotech company spread as far and wide as Regeneron could hinge so heavily on a single person, even someone as accomplished as Yancopoulos. Still, Yancopoulos and CEO Len Schleifer, age 73, have built a rapport, as evidenced by their attitude in speaking with analysts and dealing with the US government; replicating that kind of charm and goodwill is a tall order.

Competitive Landscape

As promising as Regeneron’s portfolio is, there are others to contend with for market share. Case in point: Roche’s Vabysmo growing 12% year-over-year in 2025 and 13% for Q1 2026 over Q1 2025. Keytruda continues to dominate the PD-1 inhibitor space, and its effectiveness as a first-line treatment is going to make it exceptionally difficult to unseat for Libtayo and fianlimab. Lynozyfic is entering a crowded market in myeloma on top of Elrexfio and Tecvayli/Talvey, both of which are also bispecific antibodies, meaning the edge on bispecific antibody technology we highlighted previously is starting to fade. Moreover, Johnson & Johnson’s dominance in heme-onc can’t be understated, given the success of their blockbuster Darzalex, approved in March as a combination therapy alongside Tecvayli. With many of the previous generation therapies now starting to see patents expiring, the justification for the branded treatment will need to be ironclad and may hamper their ability to pitch the drug for earlier lines of treatment. Even complement inhibition, despite its explosive upside, has established players, most notably AstraZeneca’s (AZN) Ultomiris, already approved in four indications, including gMG and PNH, grossing $4.7B in 2025 at a whopping 19% constant exchange rate growth after seven years on the market.

The interesting thing about all of these incumbents is that Regeneron’s stance, if you read through their previous earnings calls when asked about threats to Eylea and Dupixent, is that they welcome competition because they believe that more options mean a) more patients get the treatments they need, and b) the proverbial “rising tide” expands the market to all participants, i.e., thinking of market share as “zero-sum” is borderline reductionism. It’s a nice sentiment, but it also depends on the incumbent being able to continue firing on all cylinders, which may very well have been what sent Regeneron into their 2025 skid.

Valuation

Since our last major investigation and valuation, it feels like the puts and takes are fairly well balanced across the board. Lynozyfic and cemdisiran are shaping up to be big winners, while odronextamab and itepekimab have been quietly swept under the rug. The gene therapies from Alnylam and Otarmeni are showing great promise, but nex-z had a very public adverse event that has all but torpedoed its chances.

In our previous analysis, we made a pretty bold call of $1350/sh, which was 26% upside at the time, driven by increased growth in Dupixent and Libtayo while Eylea held steady, for a combined 15% growth rate in free cash flow. The reality of FY24 and FY25 was a bit more muted:

The profit share from Dupixent is growing steadily year-over-year, which is great to see as the revenues continue to compound. Eylea, however, fell well short of expectations in 2025, most likely due to the difficulties getting Eylea HD out to market in time to defend against Vabysmo and biosimilars.

With the Lynozyfic and complement franchise looking promising, the 15% growth we called for in 2027 still feels fitting, alongside the projected $180M bump to Dupixent revenues after the Sanofi revenue sharing wraps. However, given the headwinds to ophthalmology, immunology, and oncology, we don’t see much call to adjust the ranges we projected farther out: 12% for 2028-2031, 10% for 2032-2036, 8% for 2037-2041, and 3% terminal. That revises our valuation to $1230/sh, which is a small haircut from our previous analysis, but after the huge pullback, it means there’s a healthy bit of upside available at this price point. For reference, we’ve found the current range of $750/sh a great level to add to our position, as it feels like even if you take this valuation as optimistic, there’s upside as low as $900/sh.

Conclusion

After a rough twelve months, Regeneron has managed to find their footing and set themselves up well for the next phase of their development. The production issues and clinical trial setbacks were unfortunate, but that is nothing when compared to Dupixent’s continued stellar performance, the breakout potential of Lynozyfic, and the up-and-coming franchise drug that is cemdisiran, alongside their continued stockpiling of biological information and investment in future technologies. These developments signal to us that the company has rallied from their less-than-stellar 2025 and is poised to continue to lead the industry and innovate for several decades more. At $750/sh, the market is discounting the future pipeline way more aggressively than we think is justified, so we’re long and actively increasing the position as makes sense.
