data.title: Arcus Biosciences: TIGIT In The Rearview Mirror, But Market Pricing A Bit Too Aggressively For Now
data.description:
Topline Summary and Update

I am on record as an avowed optimist for the pipeline of Arcus Biosciences, Inc. (RCUS), but, in hindsight, with my eyes off the prize. I've been most excited for their promising projects within immunotherapy, specifically regarding their anti-TIGIT program, before we've seen some high-profile failures joining the graveyard of studies for this immune checkpoint. Despite that arguably catastrophic setback for RCUS, the company is actually up more than 50% since my last article, reflecting a "Buy" sentiment. Today, I want to take a look at the pivot away from immunotherapy and the prospects for the investment thesis moving forward.

Pipeline Updates

Domvanalimab

I would like to begin with what I see as the elephant in the room. I have viewed the anti-TIGIT antibody being developed by RCUS as the most important story they have had going for several years now, with highly encouraging initial data looking like they could possibly buck the trend of complete and total failure targeting TIGIT in different malignancies.

Unfortunately, an end-of-year announcement of the phase 3 STAR-221 trial in stomach cancer saw its termination due to futility. What's more, the company recently announced the discontinuation of the STAR-121 study, evaluating domvanalimab in lung cancer. This more or less means a total discontinuation of further development of this agent, and it's hard for me to imagine too many other enthusiastic attempts at exploiting TIGIT.

Casdatifan

Thankfully, RCUS has been able to pivot hard to a more established target: HIF-2a. Merck's HIF-2a inhibitor belzutifan has been making large waves in kidney cancer, first for patients with a specific mutation, and then more recently showing improvements in unselected patients with renal cell carcinoma in different settings, as well.

Belzutifan has grown to over $600 million in sales for 2025, with more to come as labels are likely to expand further. And RCUS hopes to get in on this game with casdatifan. The tolerability and initial activity of this agent have been reported from the phase 1 ARC-20 trial, with the most recent update coming out of the ASCO GU meeting earlier this year. The confirmed response rate was reported to be 35% in this heavily pretreated cohort of patients with clear-cell renal cell carcinoma.

The phase 3 PEAK-1 study, designed to evaluate the combination of casdatifan and cabozantinib vs. cabozantinib, was initiated in Q3 2025. Other trials evaluating casdatifan-based regimens without a tyrosine kinase inhibitor are also underway, with management guiding that they want to begin a phase 3 trial by the end of 2026.

Quemliclustat

The CD73 inhibitor quemliclustat is continuing early development in solid tumors, with the notable news for 2026 so far being the publication of findings from a phase 1 trial combining quemliclustat with chemotherapy for metastatic pancreatic cancer. These findings were published in Nature Medicine back in March, and they showed that the combinations being explored were tolerated well enough (considering the intensity of chemotherapy being used).

The confirmed response rate was 38% in the quemliclustat arm, and a separate arm combining it with the PD-1 antibody zimberelimab had a response rate of 25%. While there was no control arm in this study, this response rate compares well with the 23% observed in the original phase 3 Abraxane trial.

RCUS's phase 3 trial, confirming the safety and efficacy of quemliclustat-Abraxane-gemcitabine, completed enrollment late last year, with findings expected to read out in 2027.

MRGPRX2 inhibitor

RCUS is also advancing toward clinical development of a non-oncology candidate, with an inhibitor of MRGPRX2 being anticipated to move into the clinic later this year.

Financial Overview

At the end of 2025, RCUS held $222 million in cash and equivalents, as well as $759 million in marketable securities. They also had $99 million in long-term debt. The company recognized $247 million in collaboration revenue for 2025 while incurring $633 million in operating expenses. The net loss for the quarter was $353 million.

The actual cash burn for the year was $482 million, although $240 million of this was related to recognition of deferred revenue. Aside from this figure, at the 2025 burn rate, RCUS has a cash runway of between 2 and 2.5 years. This is consistent with management guidance that they should have funding sufficient to continue operations well into 2028. And this is without considering potential cost reductions related to winding down development of domvanalimab. However, it is unclear at this time what level of collaboration revenue we can expect moving forward, since the Gilead partnership was based mainly on that antibody.

Strengths and Risks

Strength—Cash and pipeline sufficient to pivot off a failure that would jeopardize other companies

Many companies have reached deep into clinical trial development with highly promising candidates, only to fail near the finish line. RCUS is one of the few exceptions I've seen where their next-in-line compound is exciting enough to keep pushing the ball forward. And even though they no longer have a major collaboration with Gilead to lean on, they are well capitalized to see casdatifan through for the foreseeable future, possibly all the way to commercialization, depending on whether they can control costs well enough.

Risk—Nothing approved yet, and no guarantees

Casdatifan and quemliclustat are definitely promising pipeline candidates and worthy successors to the momentum that had been building over the years. But it's important to keep in mind that none of their agents has reached what I would call a definitive stage of maturity. Yes, the ARC-20 trial has demonstrated pretty clear activity in pretreated kidney cancer, but whether that will translate into approval remains to be seen.

Quemliclustat's activity in pancreatic cancer is certainly encouraging, but that trial is very early. And it is not exactly a confidence builder to see response rates drop when you add zimberelimab to the mix (a sobering reminder of the severe limitations of small studies). Considering pancreatic cancer is one of our most challenging hills to climb in oncology, it's hard to have hopes set too high for this compound.

Moreover, if casdatifan is approved, RCUS will also have to clear the hurdle of demonstrating that not only is their compound worth being in the treatment armamentarium, but that it's an equal or better option than belzutifan, a drug that has already been around for years. RCUS management has highlighted the possibility of being best in class relative to belzutifan, but the oncology community is very skeptical about these possibilities in the absence of a head-to-head trial, which we are not likely to see.

Bottom-Line Summary

RCUS has had a remarkable trajectory as a clinical oncology concern, and its resilience in the face of setbacks that would be catastrophic for pretty much any other clinical-stage biotech is very impressive. I'm gratified that my past "Buy" sentiments on the stock have been affirmed, though not for the reasons I thought. Such is the nature of trying to prognosticate on anything in this space.

That said, sitting at a market cap of $3.2 billion, the hype surrounding RCUS's remaining pipeline contenders seems high, considering we're a ways off from seeing a phase 3 readout or a potential approval. RCUS management sees a $5 billion annual opportunity for casdatifan alone, but this is most likely going to be in the context of competition with big pharma for sales. The market is already pricing in substantial execution, but for me, there are a lot of question marks about the status of their operations and whether they'll be able to move casdatifan into the settings they anticipate or exactly when that will happen.

Meanwhile, while I expect they will be able to share important news throughout 2026, I suspect that they're currently sitting at a high in terms of market sentiment, and if you're interested in the RCUS story, you're likely to find a better entry point. Therefore, I am shifting sentiment to “Hold,” which says to me, “Watch and wait,” rather than indulging in a fear of missing out.

data.title: The Recession Trigger Is Active
data.description:
Bear market alert

My S&P500 (SPY) prediction for 2026 was a recessionary bear market with the AI bubble burst.

  • The recession trigger is now active - the economy is likely to slip into a recession in 2026. The specific trigger is the unfolding inflationary shock caused primarily by the energy price shock due to the prolonged closure of the Strait of Hormuz. The energy price shock is likely to ultimately produce demand destruction - and that equals recession.
  • The AI bubble burst is also unfolding, but it's in its initial stage, where investors are picking winner and losers. At this point, the losers are all companies disrupted by the AI, primarily the software companies (IGV), which include megacaps like Microsoft (MSFT). The winners are semiconductors (SMH), led by storage and memory stocks like Micron (MU). The systemic shock caused by the energy price shock is also likely to accelerate the AI bubble burst.
  • The systemic credit event. The unfolding private credit bust has affected the financial sector (XLF), although investors still don't view it as a systemic credit event. However, the energy price shock is likely to make the private credit bust a systemic credit event due to contagion.

The S&P500 (SP500) was down during the early phase of the Iran war by almost 10%, but since then it has bounced sharply - led by semiconductors, in addition to a bounce on software stocks and private credit stocks like Blue Owl (OWL). I view this bounce as a short-covering rally, which usually happens in bear markets.

Thus, my outlook for 2026 is unfolding, the trigger for a recession is active, and as the situation unfolds, the data is likely to deteriorate, and most importantly, corporate earnings for the second half of 2026 are likely to be sharply downgraded - and that's necessary for the bear market.

The trigger

Here is the fact: the Strait of Hormuz has been closed now for almost 2 months, and there is no end in sight - at this point it would be irrational to expect the Strait to fully reopen anytime soon.

As a result, we are facing the biggest energy supply shock ever, in addition to the supply shock of fertilizers, helium, and other related derivatives. Obviously, this will cause a major supply-driven inflationary shock.

Crude oil futures have already spiked to near $100 for WTI (CL1:COM) from the low 60s, 10Y yields (US10Y) have increased from 4% to 4.30%, and the Federal Funds futures have priced out two expected cuts in 2026 to higher-for-longer.

The situation has been mostly contained for now because the last pre-war tankers from Hormuz have been reaching their final destinations, but there are no tankers coming, and countries have been releasing oil from their strategic reserves, but these inventories are depleting quickly.

The situation is likely to deteriorate over the next few weeks, which means oil is likely to start rising towards $150/barrel, which is likely to result in demand destruction and a recession.

The demand destruction is already reflected via canceled airline flights due to jet fuel shortage, and this could affect tourism during the summer. This is likely to spread to other sectors of the economy as well.

As long as the Strait of Hormuz stays closed, the trigger is active.

Early confirming data

The soft data for April confirms that the unfolding inflationary recession, or stagflation.

The EU GDP is already declining, while inflation is spiking, according to the S&P Global PMI flash for April. According to Chris Williamson, Chief Business Economist at S&P Global Market Intelligence:

The eurozone is facing deepening economic woes from the war in the Middle East, presenting a major headache for policymakers. The conflict has pushed the economy into decline in April, while driving inflation sharply higher. Increasingly widespread supply shortages meanwhile threaten to dampen growth further while adding more upward pressure to prices in the coming weeks.

The US is at this point not as affected, according to the S&P Global PMI flash for April, as the US economy is less exposed to the Iran war. According to Chris Williamson, the US economy is still growing at around 1%, led by the struggling service sector, while prices are spiking. These are the early signs of stagflation.

The April PMI is broadly consistent with the economy struggling to manage annualized growth in excess of 1%, with the vast service sector acting as the principal drag. Orders for services ranging from travel and tourism to financial products barely rose as the war caused hesitancy for spending among both household and business customers, with surging prices and the prospect of higher borrowing costs acting as a further deterrent.

However, the manufacturing sector growth is due to anticipation of further shortages, similarly to what happened during the pandemics - and this is also stagflationary.

However, the sustained growth of manufacturing meanwhile seen in April comes with something of a sting in the tail, as demand for goods is being buoyed by stock building as companies scramble to secure purchases ahead of further price hikes or supply shortages. Manufacturers have increased their buying of inputs to a degree not witnessed since early 2022 as supply chain delays have also risen to the most widespread since the pandemic.

The PMI survey results are broadly consistent with other early April data, such as record low consumer confidence and a spike in LLI transportation costs.

Implications

The trigger for the inflationary recession is active - that's the Iran war and closure of the Strait of Hormuz.

Early April data confirms the severe inflationary recession pressures in the EU and some early pressures in the US.

Thus, as the Strait of Hormuz continues to stay closed, the situation is likely to severely deteriorate, which will eventually result in a sharp earnings downgrade - and that is likely to cause a deep bear market.

However, the Trump administration is aware of the situation, and the markets simply don't believe that Trump would allow for this scenario to happen - especially in an election year. Thus, the stock market is optimistic that Hormuz will fully reopen - but this has to happen immediately.

Iran wants the US to lift the blockade before negotiations start, and before reopening Hormuz, but the Trump administration knows that Iran will not abolish the nuclear program without economic pain. So, that's the game of chicken. The compromise seems very unlikely until one country starts feeling deep economic pain. So, let's assume the US starts feeling the pain first. What would the US do? Give in to Iran's demands? Or escalate militarily? I think military escalation is more likely - and that means an even bigger inflationary shock.

data.title: Digital Realty Trust Sees 6GW Of Capacity Expansion In The Future
data.description:
Digital Realty Trust, Inc. (DLR) is turning towards higher growth as demand for data center capacity ramps up, driven by AI inferencing and development. With 1.2GW under construction and a $1.8b backlog, DLR has significant visibility into the market through 2028, an appealing position to be in as the organization has plans to add 6GW of capacity over the coming years. Having signed its first large-scale hyperscaler customer for AI inferencing, DLR is focusing its attention on facilitating larger blocks of capacity to gain more exposure to this rich market, potentially delivering growth in core funds from operations.

While the growth story is compelling, I believe DLR shares have priced in a proportionate amount of the growth expected over the coming years. With no distribution growth over the last 4 years, shareholders may experience limitations to growth if the distribution continues to remain stagnant; I am downgrading DLR shares to a Hold rating with a price target of $215/share at 21.85x eFY28 EV/aEBITDA.

Digital Realty Trust Operational Update

DLR is in a unique position during a time when the data center market remains tight. The hyperscalers are pressured to build out new compute capacity to support growing demand for cloud services that are in short supply while macro constraints in power capacity persist. With the semiconductor market estimated to grow to exceed $1.3T in 2026, access to data center sites will become more critical to house the infrastructure necessary to catch up to the high-demand environment. Accordingly, a healthy proportion of the semiconductor investments will be towards AI accelerators at 30%, equating to roughly $39b.

A major challenge data center developers are facing is the significant community pushback given the size of the facilities, concerns for clean air and water associated with the emissions and recycling of data center operations, and local electricity price stability.

While this can be viewed as a negative catalyst for the data center market, it may be positive for DLR given its scaled data center footprint. In total, DLR has 3GW of data center capacity in operations with an additional 6GW under development, 1.2GW of which is currently under construction. The majority of capacity under development is in blocks of 100MW+, facilitating large-scale CSP-type customers and AI developers that may require significant amounts of capacity. In the quarter, DLR signed a 200MW lease with its first hyperscaler customer to be used for AI inferencing. To facilitate additional hyperscaler business, DLR is currently constructing multiple 200MW sites in Charlotte and Atlanta, positioning the organization to draw in more large-scale AI-related business; the 200MW site in Atlanta is expected to be completed by 2028. In addition to this, DLR acquired an 873-acre plot in the Greater Atlanta Metro to support a gigawatt data center facility, positioning the organization to support large-scale hyperscaler customers. While this is an appealing growth opportunity, DLR is in the process of securing load capacity for the site.

In total, DLR has a development pipeline of $16.5b at 100% share. A major value driver for DLR has derisked 90% of its energy exposure through customer reimbursements, with the remaining 10% hedged through 2026. This means that DLR may not experience substantial exposure to changes in electricity prices driven by market constraints and peak seasons.

DLR may be experiencing some nontraditional competition from bitcoin miners like Riot Platforms (RIOT), HIVE Digital Technologies (HIVE), and Bitdeer (BTDR) that have owned capacity and secured power load capacity at large-scale facilities. This may mean that the larger-scale customers may be less accessible for DLR when compared to larger-capacity sites.

Nonetheless, data center demand remains strong across the market, with DLR signing over $700mm in new leases, $423mm of which was attributable to DLR in q1’26. In the quarter, $98mm of leases signed were attributable to 0-1MW+ interconnection with 21% of this cohort requiring AI infrastructure.

One of the biggest value drivers DLR has going forward is enterprise adoption of AI inferencing and agentic AI. While I expect some of these workloads to be done at the edge, there may be a lot of opportunities for colocation services to support these workloads, potentially pushing enterprise customers to require larger blocks of capacity. While I anticipate enterprise AI adoption to be a slow burn given the cutover and transition to the applications, ensuring that they function as intended, I expect that the gradual adoption will flow through to DLR over the coming years, resulting in smaller capacity customers increasing their wattage.

Another appealing growth strategy is the overseas data center development to support regionalized usage and sovereign nations. With privacy and data-sharing regulations like GDPR limiting the ability to store and use data across borders, localized sites can present an appealing and diversified growth opportunity for DLR.

Digital Realty Trust Financial Position

Looking ahead to Q2 ’26, I’m forecasting DLR to generate $1.64b in operating revenue with a diluted EPS of $0.60/share. Growth in eFY26 will be delivered through multiple nodes, including increased cash renewal spreads with a target of 6.5-8.5%, strong renewals for 0-1MW leases, improved power-based occupancy, and new leases for capacity.

I’m forecasting stable top-line growth to deliver year-over-year growth in core funds from operations with incremental improvements to margins, particularly as more electricity risk is passed along to the customer. This should offset any cost risk if inflationary pressures were to persist in this tight power market. DLR currently has $5b in assets under construction and an additional $200mm held for development, for a total investment in real estate of $37b. With 3 years of visibility into leasing and 1.2GW of new capacity coming online over the coming years, I believe DLR will be well positioned to deliver consistent cash flows to support the distribution.

DLR closed Q1 ’26 with $2.4b in cash & equivalents and $18b in debt on the balance sheet for a net leverage ratio of 4.49x net debt/aEBITDA, an incremental improvement from the year-ago quarter of 4.85x.

Risks Related to Digital Realty Trust

Bull Case

DLR is expanding its large-scale data center footprint to cater to the hyperscalers and AI developers with sites scanned to 200MW+. With AI demand remaining strong, I believe DLR is primed to draw in additional hyperscaler customers for AI inferencing, particularly as it expands its regional data base footprint to service regional customers’ AI needs.

Bear Case

DLR shares exhibit a rich premium and may offer minimal upside at the current price level. The data center market is gaining new entrants with nontraditional operators developing scaled solutions, potentially taking on market share for large-scale customers for AI and cloud services. One challenge DLR may be faced with is higher EPC costs for data center site construction, which is expected to grow at 6% in 2026.

Valuation & Shareholder Value

DLR shares are currently trading near the upper end of their 3-year trading range at 25.57x EV/aEBITDA, suggesting that upside potential may be presently priced in. While I see some incremental upside to the share price, I don’t believe it justifies a Buy rating. Given the limited upside, I’m downgrading DLR shares to a Hold rating with a price target of $215/share at 21.85x eFY28 EV/aEBITDA.

DLR shares pay out an appealing distribution of $4.88/share, yielding 2.36%. While the yield and potential stability of the growing data center market may bring some appeal, the distribution has remained stagnant since 2022, making it challenging to justify buying shares for distribution growth alone. Perhaps as more capacity is built out in the coming years, management will elect to raise the distribution, but there are no guarantees.

Using the model: the valuation table above references my financial forecast in the firm’s “financial position” section and ties it to the stock’s historical trading premiums. The trading premium array is derived through the normal operating cycle, with the blue-sky scenario being the stock’s peak multiple and the gray-sky scenario being the lowest point. The target multiple aims for the midpoint, or the most likely trading range for the company’s stock. The trading multiples from there are set to a probability factor based on the likelihood of the stock trading at that premium based on its historical presence. From there, the trading multiple is tied to the probability factor to derive its relative market capitalization and relative multiple.

data.title: Huntington Ingalls: Positioned For The Largest Naval Buildout In Decades
data.description:
Investment Overview

Huntington Ingalls Industries, Inc. (HII) is one of the largest shipbuilders in the U.S., making it a clear beneficiary of the defense supercycle. While the headlines are focused on the drones and Golden Dome, shipbuilders like Huntington have received quieter news that could drive long-term growth. The Department of Defense (DoD) requested $65.8 billion for the construction of 18 warships and 16 support ships, which will make up the "Golden Fleet." This would represent the largest shipbuilding purchase since 1962. Only two companies have the capability to meet this request, and one of these is Huntington. With the company's stock trading 20% off its 52-week high going into its Q1 2026 earnings report on May 5th, it makes a compelling entry point for a long-term buy.

Huntington's competitive position is unique within the defense industry, as it designs not only nuclear-powered aircraft carriers but also nuclear-powered submarines (one of only two in the U.S.). The only other maker of nuclear-powered submarines is General Dynamics’ (GD) Electric Boat division. Huntington's infrastructure moat is further protected by a $53.1 billion backlog, providing long-term revenue visibility and protecting against economic downturns. The company also has a growing Mission Technologies segment, which generated $3 billion in 2025 revenue and saw its operating income grow ~32% YoY. The company also recently became a prime contractor on the Missile Defense Agency’s $151 billion SHIELD program, which provides additional growth opportunities for the Mission Technologies segment.

Although HII has made great strides over the past few years, the company did experience a slight downturn after Q4 results were reported in February. This decline was primarily caused by management’s 2026 shipbuilding operating margin guidance of 5.5-6.5%, which fell short of expectations. After throughput growth of 14% in 2025, investors likely expected more. Adding to near-term pressure, Q1 FCF is estimated to be negative (~$600 million) as the positive impact from reduced working capital in Q4 reverses. Although HII will face some challenges regarding near-term margin trends, these challenges are anticipated to be temporary and appear to be currently factored into investor sentiment. This near-term pressure creates a long-term opportunity to buy a primary beneficiary of the defense supercycle. That's why I'm rating the company a Buy with a 12-month price target of $427.98.

The Golden Fleet Era

Just a few days before Huntington reports its Q1 results, the DoD released an outline of what would be included in its defense budget request, which highlighted naval shipbuilding as a top priority. This includes $65.8 billion to fund the procurement of 18 warships and 16 support vessels, coming at a time when Huntington is expanding the production capacity of its yards.

The request largely represents an effort on behalf of the President to directly confront China's growing navy. With both parties in Washington supporting the idea that China's increasing naval capabilities are a threat to national security, it makes it unlikely that the ship portion of the budget will be cut. The Columbia-class submarine is still the Navy's number one acquisition priority, and Huntington has one of the two major shipyards (Newport News Shipbuilding in Virginia) in the country that can build it. Production of the Virginia-class Block V submarines is currently underway at its facility, and the company has received contract modifications for two additional submarines in 2025.

Revenue increased by 11.2% in 2025 at the company's Ingalls Shipbuilding division in Pascagoula due to increased volume for both surface combatants and amphibious assault ships. In addition, a recently signed memorandum of agreement with HD Hyundai Heavy Industries is intended to find opportunities to increase capacity at the yard. The AUKUS agreement will also add a significant long-term demand vector for the company, as Virginia-class submarines will need to be produced continuously to keep up with transfer requirements to Australia.

Beyond the DoD budget request, the company expects revenue to grow through increased throughput, with a projected 15% for 2026 after last year's growth of 14%. Additionally, more than 40 ships are either under construction or undergoing modernization at Huntington's yards. The company also invested over $400 million in capital improvements during 2025 to modernize infrastructure and expand capacity. These investments have laid the groundwork for improved margins at each of the company's yards as utilization rates continue to increase. This all comes at a perfect time with expected budget increases, making today's risk/reward profile favorable.

Mission Technologies

Many reports on the company have primarily focused on how well the company can build ships, which makes sense considering those operations make up the majority of its revenue. However, the company's Mission Technologies segment is emerging as a significant growth driver.

Mission Technologies’ revenue was $3 billion in 2025, and operating income increased by ~32% YoY to $153 million. That level of operating leverage is unusual for a legacy defense company. The segment also received a prime award on the Missile Defense Agency’s SHIELD contract, an IDIQ vehicle with a $151 billion ceiling for directed energy, cyber defense, microelectronics, and data operations. While IDIQ contracts represent theoretical maximum earnings for each contractor involved, obtaining a prime position allows Mission Technologies to capture some of the high-priority DoD spending.

Outside of SHIELD, Huntington is currently the world’s largest manufacturer of unmanned underwater vehicles. This includes supplying over 750 REMUS UUVs, delivery of the Lionfish small uncrewed undersea vehicles, and a successful deployment of the Yellow Moray UUV from a Virginia-class submarine. Given the DoD's shift towards using autonomous systems for combat, the combination of HII’s developed manufacturing capabilities and deployed fleet creates both a competitive data and customer relationship advantage. This makes the Mission Technologies segment underrated compared to the overall business, with signs of operating leverage and a clear long-term growth path.

Earnings Preview

The company is scheduled to report its Q1 2026 results on May 5. Consensus EPS estimates for the quarter are ~$3.71, which is a slight decrease from $3.79 YoY. With negative FCF guidance already announced, the headline financials are not likely to be the biggest drivers of the stock.

The first important metric is throughput growth, which is expected to reach ~15% in FY 2026, up from 14% growth in FY 2025. On the Q4 call, CEO Chris Kastner stated that the company expects to increase outsourcing by 30% in 2026 as part of expansion. Any comments regarding the company's ability to achieve this throughput level in Q1 2026 will be significant to investors and could play a big part in determining full-year margin expectations.

Revenue and operating income growth at Mission Technologies should be another point of interest. During 2025, Mission Technologies' revenue increased by 3.6%, while the operating income for that segment rose by 32%. Should this momentum continue into Q1 2026, then it shows that Mission Technologies can scale efficiently rather than just win contracts.

The total backlog amount is also important. At the end of FY 2025, the company had a total backlog value of $53.1 billion. With the Golden Fleet budget request and SHIELD IDIQ both eligible for task order awards, any notes around this from management could be impactful. With consistent earnings beats throughout the last four quarters, the market is likely expecting numbers better than consensus. This makes commentary more important overall, especially regarding throughput growth and Mission Technologies momentum.

Valuation

Huntington is priced at ~20.6x forward EPS. For comparison, Lockheed Martin (LMT) trades at ~17.2x forward EPS. One might initially assume that Huntington appears to be overpriced in relation to Lockheed, but this does not fully account for Huntington's growth rate and potential. With Mission Technologies' emergence and the Golden Fleet potential, it places Huntington's growth ceiling higher with optionality for big deals. In contrast, Lockheed will likely face challenges as it approaches the production limit of its F-35 program.

As such, I believe that a valuation of 21x the company's FY 2027 EPS estimate of $20.38 would provide a reasonable price target of ~$427.98, representing 19% upside. Given Huntington's low-risk demand environment, fourteen years of increasing dividends, and potential access to the largest U.S. Naval construction project in recent history, I do not believe that this represents an excessively high valuation.

Risks

The biggest risk factor is margin timing, with many investors already disappointed with guidance for margins of 5.5-6.5%. If the company does not achieve its expected throughput growth, then margins could continue to disappoint. Shipbuilding has fluctuations with contract timing, program mix, and labor availability, which could cause variability quarter by quarter. Huntington disclosed the expected negative Q1 FCF, reminding the market that cash generated by shipbuilding operations will be behind earnings in some quarters. If this struggle continues, it could continue to hurt margins and the growth outlook.

The budget for the Golden Fleet is still a request; therefore, Congress could reduce or completely eliminate funding for shipbuilding. However, the company still has long-term contracted commitments to build Columbia-class submarines and Virginia-class attack submarines, which provide long-term revenue visibility. This keeps the backlog intact, regardless of what happens to the budget request.

Conclusion

Huntington is one of a handful of defense companies in the U.S. that produces unique items for the Navy and has a footprint in advanced technologies through Mission Technologies. This is all while the geopolitical environment drives the largest naval procurements in over six decades. The recent pull-back in the shares now creates an attractive risk/return scenario for long-term investors, who can potentially benefit from these catalysts at a discount.

While the raw numbers are likely to reflect a relatively weak Q1 due to negative FCF and a YoY EPS decline, the significance of the report will largely be in the comments made by management. This will surround progress toward higher throughput rates, the trajectory of the company's backlog, and the rate of adoption of new Mission Technologies products. Positive commentary in these areas could support a re-rating, where concerns about margins are overshadowed by increased long-term growth potential. This is why I'm rating the stock a Buy, with a 12-month price target of $427.98.

data.title: The S&P 500 Clocks A New Record High As Geopolitics Hold Index Back From Rising Higher
data.description:
The second-longest ever winning streak for the S&P 500 (SPX) was broken on Monday, 20 April 2026. The streak had endured for 13 days, one short of the all-time record.

While the streak was broken, all was not bad for Wall Street bulls, who could celebrate the index hitting a new all-time record high closing value of 7,165.08 on Friday, 24 April 2026.

The streak largely came about as a result of the ceasefire in the Iran War, which was at risk of ending before President Trump announced an extension during the past week. The market's new highs came in response to that event, the prospect of diplomatic talks between the U.S. and Iran soon resuming, and the strength of earnings out of the market's technology sector.

All these things kept the trajectory of the S&P 500 running along the bottom end of the redzone forecast range we added to the alternative futures chart back in February, as shown in its latest update. That range assumes investors would be focused on the current quarter of 2026-Q2, which they certainly have been, even with the disruption of the Iran war geopolitical event.

The centerline of the redzone forecast range provides a reasonable estimate of where the S&P 500 would have gone in the absence of the Iran war. From Friday, 17 April 2026, through Friday, 24 April 2026, the index has ranged between 2.5 and 3.0% below that trajectory, which is to say the geopolitical event is keeping the S&P 500 from rising even higher.

What happens next for the S&P 500 will be determined by investor reactions to the random onset of new information. Here are the market-moving headlines that affected the trajectory of the index during the week that was.
